content:
The **likelihood principle**—infer θ from how probable the observed data is under competing parameter values—crystallized in early 20th-century statistics. **Fisher** (1912–1922) developed **maximum likelihood estimation** as a systematic method, proving optimality properties and introducing **Fisher information** to quantify curvature of ℓ(θ) near θ̂.

**Gauss** (1809) used least-squares fits that later aligned with Gaussian MLE for the mean. **Bernoulli** and **Laplace** estimated binomial proportions long before the MLE name existed—sample proportions maximize binomial likelihood.

Mid-century, **Wald** and others formalized consistency and asymptotic normality of MLE. **Neyman** and **Egon Pearson** contrasted likelihood with hypothesis testing, while **Jeffreys** and the Bayesian school treated likelihood as the data term in posterior inference rather than a standalone estimator.

In machine learning, **logistic regression** (Nelder–Wedderburn GLM framework, 1972) and **backpropagation** (1986) made numerical MLE routine: minimize −ℓ(θ) with gradients. Modern deep learning—softmax cross-entropy, language-model next-token NLL—is large-scale MLE under flexible parametric models, optimized with stochastic gradient descent.

Today MLE remains the default frequentist point-estimation framework and the probabilistic reading of most training losses in Gen AI pipelines.

timeline.label: Least squares (Gauss, 1809)
timeline.description: Fitting errors by minimizing squared deviation—later recognized as Gaussian MLE for the mean.

timeline.label: MLE formalized (Fisher, 1910s–1920s)
timeline.description: argmax L(θ) with asymptotic efficiency and Fisher information for standard errors.

timeline.label: GLMs and logistic regression (1970s)
timeline.description: Exponential-family models trained by maximizing log-likelihood via iteratively reweighted least squares.

timeline.label: Deep learning NLL losses (1990s–present)
timeline.description: Cross-entropy and token NLL scale MLE to neural networks with mini-batch gradient descent.
